## The Aim

Software Engineering is one of the most sought-after skills in the modern era, and for good reason. Just about everything tech-related involves some sort of software, making the demand for people who can create it pretty high. That being said, my aim in this series of posts is not to brag about how awesome software engineers are (although we are pretty cool). Instead, I want to take you through the journey of becoming a software engineer. What this boils down to is a number of (what I hope will be) bite-sized posts that bring you up to speed on the knowledge, skills and attributes required to be a modern software engineer. 

## Software Engineering

Although I mentioned that software engineering is a highly desirable skill, it seems as though many people I encounter still don't really understand what being a software engineer entails. I guess that's understandable considering the large number of positions which get lumped into the category of software engineering. You may hear terms like developer, solutions architect and cloud engineer being thrown around, and all of these positions could potentially be replaced with the title of software engineer.

The old statement that a software engineer turns caffeine into code isn't far wrong. I'm not quite sure why the allure of coffee is irresistible to us but a typical day involves caffeine and code aplenty. In fact, a previous colleague of mine could rack up sixteen cups on a bad day. Nonetheless, as a software engineer, expect to eat, sleep and breathe code.

Code is (generally) the final output of the software engineering process and generating code - in one way or another - tends to be the main task of a software engineer. Depending on experience and technical skill, this may range from designing the entire system at a high level, to producing part of the source code itself.

Contrary to popular opinion we can't fix your printer - well, we probably can but we don't want to. We also don't know why your Windows machine keeps crashing. After all, why aren't you running some arbitrarily obscure distribution of Linux like us? 

I'm getting a bit ahead of myself here so don't panic if the last sentence was a little confusing. The point is that software engineers produce code. There are quite literally thousands of tools and techniques that are used to accomplish this task and we'll get to a lot of the more popular ones in later posts. I hope I didn't ramble on for too long and that you at least have a fairly good idea of what a software engineer does. Look out for the next post in this series where I discuss the beginning of the journey to becoming a software engineer.

UPDATE: The next post in the series is now live where I talk about [the qualities required to be a software engineer](/posts/2020-05-09-who_can_be_a_software_engineer/).

_Jason Parry_
